subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: he devises the concept of a meme (from the Greek word mimesis, meaning 'imitation'), which is a discrete unit of thought, so to speak (ibid., p.206). He suggests that a meme is directly analogous to a gene, on the basis that it has a key property which is the ability to replicate itself
rationale: Paper names the discrete-copyable-unit / meme-as-replicator premise as the foundational move of memetics it sets out to assess — exact statement of the replicator ontology.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: ultimately, there is no empirical test which will enable us to distinguish between relative copy infidelity and meme diversity. The elements of wish-fulfilment and volition involved in cultural assimilation have led to many interpretations of cultural change ('evolution', if you must) in Lamarckian, rather than Darwinian terms
rationale: Paper's core attack: the discrete-replicator assumption is empirically untestable and cultural transmission is reconstructive/elective (Lamarckian), not copy-based — directly critiques the replicator ontology.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: the concept of a meme depends upon the notion of pure information or pure ideas; i.e., ideas as discrete entities, with linguistic and syntactic independence... The essential formal connection of a meme with its respective medium is never taken into material consideration in this theory, which hence becomes an ideology of pure content
rationale: Paper argues the discrete-unit/'pure information' assumption is incoherent because it severs the idea from its material medium — a direct critique of the replicator ontology.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: There is no pure content of meaning, such as is suggested in the notion of a meme, independent of a signifying structure and process.
rationale: Paper deploys Saussurean linguistics to deny that meaning exists as a discrete transmissible unit — the replicator ontology contradicted on first principles.

subject: concept:replicator-ontology
quote: memetics presupposes that ideas are like free-floating entities, without formal/textual specificities (i.e., without a history, and without a code)... memetics gives to memes the status of metaphysical absolutes - as autonomous entities in their own right
rationale: Paper's closing verdict: the discrete-autonomous-replicator premise reifies ideas into metaphysical absolutes — the replicator ontology rejected outright.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: the minds represented by the parentheses are not in actuality simply passive transmitters or receivers, but also possess consciousness and intelligence - factors whose influence is not represented in the model, and which are neither required by the theory.
rationale: Paper insists receivers actively reflect and resist rather than passively absorb memes — the active cognitive-defense (epistemic vigilance) position, against the easy-infection narrative.

subject: concept:epistemic-vigilance
quote: By virtue of the analogy with biological processes (and only by virtue of this) there is no role left for conscious criticism in the reception and transmission of memes - there are only levels of susceptibility, and degrees of (secondary) variation.
rationale: Paper objects that memetics erases the receiver's conscious critical scrutiny, reducing people to susceptibility levels — implicitly arguing for epistemic vigilance the theory ignores.

_note: Strongest hit by far: replicator-ontology — the paper is a sustained attack on the discrete-copyable-unit / self-replication premise (pp.1, 9, 19, 22-23), arguing transmission is reconstructive and meaning is medium-bound (Saussure). epistemic-vigilance appears only as a negative-space argument (receivers possess consciousness/'conscious criticism' the theory brackets out) — no Mercier, no 'mental immune systems', so medium confidence. neutral-drift-baseline is near-miss: the paper discusses 'chance'/'isomorphic' random variation and drift toward unrecognizability (fn.10, p.11) but never the Yule/rich-get-richer null model — not anchorable. meme-ecosystem-competition touched only via 'mutually compatible gangs' forming packages (p.21) and 'survival of the fittest ideas' (p.20), but meme-vs-meme competition for shared attention is not developed. paradox-of-virality, superspreader-dynamics, and algorithm-as-selection-engine are entirely absent (this is a 2011 philosophy-of-science critique with no digital-platform content).
